Hey everyone, this is Russell and welcome you guys to the webinar for this morning for you guys and this afternoon for me. Hope you guys do an awesome and... Cool, you guys said you can hear me already. Awesome, good to know. Okay, well, I appreciate you guys getting on the webinar today. This one will be a little bit of a shorter one. There's not a lot I'm teaching here, I just wanna kind of give some concepts because we had a lot of people email me asking me questions about how to structure the PowerPoint slides and how that part works. So I'm gonna go over some of that. If you've done presentations in the past, a lot of this would probably be just review for you guys, but if not, this should hopefully help you get your stories from the paper into like a PowerPoint format. And so before I begin though, I wanna apologize for yesterday. I'm sorry that I had to miss the webinar, especially such last minute thing. It was... I wish I could tell you the whirlwind of the last week that I've had. We were doing a big webinar last Thursday. We promoted it. We got about 5,000 people to register for it. And the night before the webinar, I was working late trying to get everything ready for it and finished. And all of a sudden, all my servers went down. So all our websites were down and we were freaking out and the guy who runs the servers was in the hospital with his wife and it was about five or six hours of the office trying to get servers back up. So finally got them up online. Then we had to call all our staff to get them to start working, get it midnight to get everything finished for the next day. And we were working about four or five o'clock in the morning and then at five... Actually four o'clock when I got done. Four o'clock I went out to get my car and it was raining ice and my car was completely sealed shut with ice and about a fourth of an inch of ice around the whole thing. So I had to like yank on my door and I finally popped the door open, got inside of my car and I turned the heater on and sat there for half an hour for 30 minutes, defrosting my windows before. I finally there was about a hole about the size of my hand that got defrosted on the windshield and I was able to look through that and drive home at 4, 30 or 5 o'clock in the morning through that little tiny hole and got home, fell asleep, woke up a couple hours later to do the webinar, woke up and my voice was completely gone. And when you got 5,000 people on a webinar, as I'm sure you guys can imagine, that's supposed to be a big payday, right? And so I did not want to cancel that webinar for life, but I couldn't even pronounce, I can say anything. And then so finally we shifted the webinar, that was supposed to be Thursday, we shifted it till Tuesday and because of that we got a whole bunch more people to register because they were all excited about why is this thing being moved and so we got even more people registered. So that was yesterday. So I started doing the webinar about two minutes into the webinar, go to webinar, pop up saying completely maxed out, there's nobody else can get on the line, you used all your thousand spots. And so I'm doing the webinar and about 20 minutes into it, go to webinar, I actually kicked me off of it and I was out and I took me, I spent five minutes trying to get logged back in and I could not get back in and kept telling me the webinar was full. And so I'm like freaking out because I got a thousand people waiting to hear me talk to them and I can't even get on there, it's finally had run to another office and somebody else in our office is already on. So I jumped on there, did the whole webinar, finished it off as much energy as I could, my voice was every minute by minute getting softer and weaker and weaker. And then as soon as that was done, then we had a live training for those members. So we do the webinar right afterwards. And by the end of that, my voice was so gone and then I had our webinar and I was like, I couldn't even tell you, I couldn't even, I could barely make a noise. So I appreciate you guys allowing me to move until today. My voice is still kind of, it's kind of in and out today, but at least you can hear me hopefully. So that's my fun crazy little bit. And I was watching the news up here and I heard that down in Brisbane, it's raining like crazy. So some of you guys are probably buried right now too in some crazy weather. So I hope everything's okay for everybody down there and hope you guys are having any issues at all. So, all right. And then the last thing, I know there's a couple of people that have sent me homework that I haven't had a chance to get back to just because last week's been crazy, but again, there won't be too much new after this session. So I'll go back through over those and get back to you guys as quick as I can. All right. So I'm just going to jump right in. If you guys have questions along the way, please let me know and I'll address those questions. So most of you guys, I assume, have probably done some kind of presentation in the past. There's two core software programs that we use to do presentations. The first one is PowerPoint if you're like a Windows user and the other one's Keynote if you're a Mac user. So I like PowerPoint personally because I can create things really, really fast. I've just been using PowerPoint forever. So I do most of my presentations in there. This presentation that I'm showing you guys was actually done on Keynote, but it was done on the iPad version of Keynote. And I'm not sure if you guys have ever used the iPad version of Keynote, but it is one of the neatest, most amazing programs I've ever seen. It's by far my favorite software program to create presentations on. I think that the presentations I did in Australia when I was out there with you guys, I created both of those on Keynote. And it's just like the fastest, easiest way I've ever created presentations. But then I don't like, I personally don't like the real version of Keynote. So I do them all on my iPad that I email that presentation to my real computer, my Mac computer, and then I can edit it there, but I don't like editing it there nearly as much. It's a lot harder to use. So for me, those are kind of two options. I'm guessing most of you guys, if you're a Mac person, you're probably using Keynote. If you are a Windows person, you're probably going to use PowerPoint. I know there's other programs online nowadays. There's online versions of presentation software and stuff. I've never person used them. I try to make things pretty simple and easy, and that's kind of how I do my stuff. And it's always worked really, really good for us. Okay. So the presentation that I'm showing you guys right now, this is actually the new, invisible funnel I'm creating for dot com secrets. And it's not completely finished yet. As you'll see, there's a lot of holes in these slides and stuff. But this is mostly finished. I'd say it's probably 80, 85% done. This is one that I'm hoping... I was trying to get it recorded this week, but just this week turned out a little crazier than I planned. So hopefully next week, I'll be recording this entire presentation. And this is when I'm going to have transcribed and give you guys, so you can kind of see how I do my transitions and my clothes and things like that. So I'm just going to go through this PowerPoint and show you guys the things that are important to me when I'm creating a presentation, and some of the things that kind of drive me crazy about when people make presentations. So I'm pretty picky about stuff. And so because of that, I'm just going to kind of go through this. So the very beginning, I like having kind of like the title of my presentation and a picture of me, because especially through a webinar like this, the people that you're talking to, they can't see you. They have no idea who you are. And so it's important to have some kind of picture just so that they have someone to relate to. And that can be your picture. It can be the picture of one of your students, whoever it is, but some kind of picture so they can relate to who you are. I can't tell you how important that is in this kind of a webinar, because again, because they can't see you, you lose a lot of that personal, attractive character story building stuff. When they can see a picture of you and your family, then it helps a lot. So I use a lot of pictures, but I use them very strategically too. So I'll kind of show you that. So again, I have my title, my presentation, and my picture. And then I like starting out with Who's This Webinar 4, because it helps kind of let people know that they're in the right spot. But you notice one thing, and this is kind of my theme throughout this entire thing I'm talking about with you guys, is you notice that I'm not ever using sentences. I use one or two or three words just to illustrate and to kind of pull me along where I'm trying to go. The second you start using big, a lot of bullet points, a lot of words and things like that, that's when you will shift out of storytelling mode and you'll shift into your reading mode. And that's the worst possible thing you can do in a webinar. The second you're reading your slides, you will kill all the momentum, you'll kill everything that gets people excited and they're not going to want to stay until the end. That's really important to understand. So notice I never use sentences, I always just use words to kind of drive my thoughts. So if I was doing this slide, I'd say, now who is this webinar for? This webinar is for people who are in a startup. I know there's a lot of people right now who are trying to start new businesses, you've got an idea, you've got something you want to create out there, and you just don't know how to market and get that idea out there. So if you're in the startup business, then this is perfect for you. The second person is bloggers. We've got a lot of bloggers who watch this webinar who have a blog, they may have a following right now or they want to get a following, but they have no idea how in the world to monetize that, how to turn that into money. And so if you're a blogger, this presentation is going to help you understand how to monetize your blog. This is also created for authors and information publishers. If you've written a book or an e-book or you want to write a book, this is going to show you how to monetize your content, your information. If you have an e-commerce store, you're selling physical products online, this is going to help really be the big breaking point for you as you understand how to really monetize the customers that are coming to you. And if you've got a business that's offline but you want to get online, like let's say you're a chiropractor or a dentist or you've got something like that, and you know the internet's where it's at, you just don't know how in the world to monetize the internet, then this webinar is for you. So you see, I use just words to remind me of what it is, but then I just go completely off script and I add a little bit. Now this is important, when I do sales presentations, I stick to a script very, very close. When I'm doing an invisible funnel type presentation, the story and the energy and excitement is much more important than a script. So I just want to kind of differentiate that. A couple of questions coming in. Okay, someone said, do these points come in a mouse click or come in all at once? It's up to you. I personally have each one come in one at a time. So start first, I talk about it, then I click it and then bloggers, and then authors, information publishers, e-commerce stores, businesses wanting to online. I like doing that one at a time because otherwise people read ahead. And if I'm talking about a startup person, I don't want the e-commerce, I don't want the people reading about all this stuff, I want them listening to me. That's the other big problem I see with, especially people trying to sell from stage, is they'll have so much text on their slides that people are reading their slides. And when they're reading your slides, they're not looking and focusing on you. And so that's why it's so key. Again, you want to focus on your words, you want people listening. And so you just want words that spark what you're talking about, your talking points. Again, here I talk about my backstory. Now again, I like using pictures but not too many. This is more than I normally come to know it, but I was illustrating two concepts. One that I was a wrestler in college and one that I was not a very smart kid. I had a horrible GPA. And so that was kind of what I did with that one. You just use one picture, very small, and then talk about a backstory. I'll show an image that I kind of liked. And then I'm just going to tell my story throughout here. I'm not going to have bullet points necessarily for every single point of it. I'm just going to show this and kind of talk my way through it. Here's my beautiful family. I'm going to talk about them for a while. This is not a cute family, you guys. I sure love them. Yeah, there's my family. So again, all these personal things I'm sharing to kind of build that reciprocity with the end listener. I want them relating to me and bonding to me because that's... Because at the end of this, they've got to decide if they're going to... If they're going to let me build them or not for the being on the webinar. If they don't like me, then at the end of the thing they're like, no, I don't like Russell. You can't build me. But if they like me and they see my family and my kids, they're going to be basically taking money out of your kids' pockets. Most people aren't going to do that. So that's why I think it's important, these kind of presentations to share with people your personal life, share with them as much as you're willing to share, show them pictures of your family, things like that. So I tell my story. This is like my story downs, is like the mistakes that I made. And then here's my story up here. It's the good stuff that happened. And then this is all the other benefits of what happened. So this is just me telling my backstory. So I'm just showing pictures of the core elements so that they can kind of they're kind of drawn in there. But I'm not driving thousands of bullet points about everything. Just showing the picture and then I'm going to tell the story on my own. All right. Then I start transitioning over to what I'm going to teach them. Okay. If you remember last week we talked about, excuse me, we talked about Kelmer, you're going to teach them and then you teach it to them. Then you told them what you just taught them, right? So here's what I kind of talking about this concept. So I'm illustrating like this is where this whole concept came from and why you should care. Okay. So I'm talking about basically this one, how a lot of you guys have seen businesses online and you think, you know, how they work. The problem is you're only seeing the tip of the iceberg. And so you go and you model that and you, you create the tip of the iceberg as well, you go out there and try to sell it and what happens. You don't make that much money. And you're saying, my site looks just like his. What's wrong? What's the difference? And what you're missing is all the stuff in the background. There's a big difference between $10,000 of your business and $10 million of your business. They look the same on the outside, but there's all this stuff that's happening underground. And that's what we're going to be talking about. You know, I didn't understand this when I first got online. So here's my first business. Okay. I started, I put up a website and I started driving traffic to it and I made some money. Okay. But this was a $10,000 of your business. That was about all I was able to make with it. Let me tell you a story about an offer. Here's my parable. Yeah. And I'm going to tell the story about Domino's guy. The guy, I'm sure he has heard that story. The guy who started Domino's pizza. Um, I see me as have Domino's down there in Australia, but I have no idea. Um, cause like, I think he said Burger King, but I went down there and you have like Burger Jack or something. It wasn't Burger King. I looked like Burger King, uh, when I went in there and I bought, I bought my breakfast, uh, it wasn't a Burger King breakfast. And you guys call it Brekkies instead of breakfast. So you may not have Domino's. I don't even know. Uh, but if you don't know the story about Domino's, there was this like pizza place that was going bankrupt and these two brothers bought it and they, it was called Dominix and these guys brought it, bought it from a family and the family was like, you can have a, you have to change the names. They called it Domino's and they kept trying and it was failing. So one of the brothers was like, I'm out of here and he took off and left. And the other brother was sitting there with his like failing pizza place and how can I blow this thing up? And so he, uh, he had the idea to, to basically go and, uh, and change the offer and turn the offer to a fresh hot pizza in 30 minutes or it's free. Now this is big, uh-huh. And he create that offer, put it out there and boom, his restaurant exploded. And they started franchising around the whole country, maybe the world. I don't even know. And it actually at one point became the biggest pizza chain in the world, beating out pizza. And then, uh, and, uh, then some of the delivery guys actually hit people in cars and so because of that, they'd take their offer away and it dropped down now they're underneath pizza. It's just kind of going through your outline you guys built before and just kind of putting this thing in here. Now I'm not sure how many words are in this whole thing. There can't be more than maybe 500 words in the entire presentation. And that's important, okay? Because if you guys try to script out every single word of every single slide, you're going to be doing this for the next six months or longer. And I don't want that. I want just a slide where it has a headline. Okay, and I would make sure this is probably me being kind of picky, but make your headline fonts the same on every single slide. Okay, those mine are the same throughout. It drives me crazy when people change their fonts from slide to slide to slide. Because I always have my headline font one thing and then my side font another thing. And my slides are all very simple. Headline, couple of bullet points and maybe a picture. And that's it. Okay? You'll notice if I go deeper, I think. Couple of the secrets I haven't flushed out quite as much. You guys are curious now, you want to watch this presentation, don't you? But I get it done all of a sudden to you guys, the videos, if you do want to watch it. I think you'll like it. So let's see. Yeah, I haven't put all the bullet points yet. That's the biggest thing. Okay, so like this. So like this is like the most complicated slide should be. Headline, bullet point, bullet point, image. Okay, and you don't have to have an image, you can use bullet points. But that's about it. Okay, don't over complicate it. Someone who's related to me, I'll just say that, did a presentation for me at one of our events and they made their PowerPoint slide. And it was insane. It had like every word, the word spun around and then these little images of morph and do things and jump around. And they had each of these bullet points would like fly around and come back in. And every single slide, every transition, they had spent so much time. Every transition was different. Every slide was different. And all that stuff is all nice and well, but it's annoying. Like if you sit through a presentation for an hour or two hours watching that and every single time these things are spinning and moving, it's just, it'll drive your audience crazy. So make it very simple. When you come to a slide, boom, the headline's already there. You push button number one, first thing pops up, button two, second thing pops up. Just having very simple transitions. There are cool transitions, but you don't have to use them. You have my permission to not use them. Yeah, I mean guys, this is pretty much it. So then I go through, keep teaching each of my seven secrets. Oh, so here's my version. So this is me sketching it out. So pair a bowl step by step example. And then I come down and I do secret number two. That's the number two. And again, then I, actually, I'm just going to do this. This is easier. Boom. Number two, my parable. Number two, my step by step. And number two, my example. And that's how I started doing first try guys, is building out the structure of the framework. Start putting them in, boom, boom, boom. Don't worry about pictures and transitions or anything right now. Just focus on getting the content in there and then save it and leave it. And then come back the next day and look at it and give it, okay, do I like the order, I like the secret first? Or should this maybe be secret number two? Maybe this one, that's what I did a lot online. I switched my secrets around like five or six times. And I had a couple of ones where I was just like, this is good, this is good, but it's not really a secret. Instead, I'm going to include it over here inside of this section right here. Okay. Now I just go back and forth a couple of times so you have the outline completely dialed in. You know what's going to be in each step. You kind of have an example of story. And the second step, I would come in and then I start adding like different pictures and elements you want to add in for all the major things. That way you're kind of start visually, start building it out. And then the last event is kind of doing a final run through and just making sure that you have the bullet points you need to help you tell your story. Okay. The worst thing is when you're recording something that's going live, a lot of times, at least for me, I get nervous. I forget parts about my story. So I start thinking, what are the most important things I want to make sure that I tell in this parable? I want to make sure that I talk about the first part. So let's just say, like the parable I told you guys, the story I told you guys today, I told you this about me losing my voice. So it started with 5000 people registered for a webinar and we stayed, then our servers crashed, servers crashed, stayed up till 4 a.m. My car was frozen, frozen shut. So it was frozen shut next morning, no voice. And then moved webinar till Thursday, next outlines again. Okay. So that's how I would structure that if I was into that secret and I have a little picture of my car frozen or something. And I say, look, before I tell you the secret, I just want to tell you kind of a funny story about this. We have this webinar and I would start telling you. Okay. You know, it's like, I'm not telling you, I'm not writing out a whole story. I'm just giving myself, these are mostly bullet points for me to remember where I'm moving throughout my story. And that's the most important part. Because if you tell the whole story on your PowerPoint, it's going to be boring. If you don't have the talking points to move you through it, you're going to forget those things. You're going to be so mad, afterwards you're going to be like, oh, I forgot the most important part of the story. I forgot to tell you about my car frozen shut because that was the key to this whole thing or whatever it was. But it's very important to have the talking points in there for each of the sections, for the secret also, for the steps, and then for the examples. It's just kind of showing, having the talking points where we want to make sure you cover. Okay. But you guys, other than that, if you look at any of my presentations, this is what they almost all look like. Okay. They're so simple. We just keep plugging little things like, you have like a picture and then maybe I'll make part of the headline red because they'll want something to stick out. That's about it. Nothing more fancy than that. And the only other thing I like doing, you can see right here, I like having a nice background slide, but just something really simple. Usually I find an image, I get my logo on it, and you just create an image. And I don't know how to create an image. I don't know if you guys have tech people or programmers or somebody can hire an ODesk. That's all we basically do. So I create an image and then right here, when I'm working on the slide, I do format background and then click on picture. And then I can search for an image that'll become my background. Otherwise they give me these funny ones. I only have, let's see. Ooh, I'm gonna make a jellyfish to the background. So now here's the background for all my slides. Okay. I obviously don't really want that, but I did anyway. Okay. Or you just do gradient fill, make it a different color, but I almost really haven't just white. And then if I have a logo, maybe just put my logo down at the bottom right hand corner. I do that a lot of times. In fact, that's what I did on this presentation right here. See, I said a white presentation, I just took my logo right here. And I just copied it. As far as I can get on this one, come back here, just paste my logo. And I just copied on every single slide. Okay. But that's a good looking PowerPoint slide. You don't need much more than that to keep people's attention because it's your stories, it's your energy, it's your excitement. That also keeps people sucked in, it keeps them moving through the process. Does that make sense? Do you have any questions right now at this point?
